perturbation.original_text: The Company shall protect, indemnify and hold harmless each of Nantz Communications and Nantz against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, arising out of, or in any way connected with such Endorsement, in any advertising or promotional materials furnished by or on behalf of the Company, actions or omissions of the Company or any claim or action for personal injury, death or other cause of action involving alleged defects in Ashworth Products, including but not limited to indemnification of reasonable legal expenses incurred in defense of all such claims.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall endeavor to protect, indemnify, and hold harmless each of Nantz Communications and Nantz against expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments, and costs connected with such Endorsement, arising from advertising/promotional materials, Company actions/omissions, or claims for personal injury/death related to Ashworth Products; indemnification of legal expenses will be considered.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall protect, indemnify and hold harmless' with 'shall endeavor to protect, indemnify, and hold harmless' weakens the obligation. Also, 'including but not limited to indemnification of reasonable legal expenses incurred in defense of all such claims' is replaced by 'indemnification of legal expenses will be considered,' which makes indemnification discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 outlines specific rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. By weakening the indemnification language to 'endeavor' and making legal expense coverage discretionary ('will be considered'), the modified text introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as conflicting with the clear obligations imposed by California law regarding the scope and enforcement of indemnity agreements. Specifically, it contradicts the expectation of a clear and definite obligation to defend and indemnify, as the modified language allows for subjective interpretation and potential avoidance of these responsibilities.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 2778 regarding indemnity agreements. The original text states the Company 'shall protect, indemnify and hold harmless,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'shall endeavor to protect, indemnify, and hold harmless,' introducing ambiguity. Additionally, the original text specifies 'indemnification of reasonable legal expenses incurred in defense of all such claims.' The changed text replaces this with 'indemnification of legal expenses will be considered,' making indemnification discretionary. Per CIV 2778, 'the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears,' implying a standard of definite obligation. By weakening the language, the modified text introduces uncertainty and contradicts the expectation of a clear obligation to defend and indemnify.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, advertising and product liability insurance each with limits no less than $5,000,000 and within thirty (30) days from the date hereof, the Company will submit to Nantz Communications a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Nantz Communications and Nantz as insured parties, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Nantz Communications at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will strive to provide advertising and product liability insurance, with limits around $5,000,000, and will make an effort to submit a policy or certificate of insurance naming Nantz Communications and Nantz as insured parties, requesting the insurer to provide notice of termination or material modification.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to provide and maintain' to 'will strive to provide' makes the insurance provision less mandatory. Replacing 'no less than $5,000,000' with 'around $5,000,000' introduces ambiguity regarding the coverage amount. Altering 'requiring that the insurer shall not terminate' to 'requesting the insurer to provide notice' weakens the obligation for advance notice of policy changes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 381 requires that insurance policies specify the parties between whom the insurance contract is made and the risk insured against. By using vague language like 'strive to provide' and 'around $5,000,000', the modified text introduces uncertainty about the company's obligation to actually secure and maintain specific levels of insurance coverage, potentially leading to a policy that doesn't adequately protect the named insured parties as required by California law. The change from 'requiring' to 'requesting' reduces the legally binding force of the notification requirement, possibly leaving Nantz unprotected if the insurance is modified or canceled.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, INS 381 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Insurance Code - INS DIVISION 1. GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 1. THE CONTRACT [100 - 679.75] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 4. The Policy [380 - 460] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) ARTICLE 1. Definition and Scope [380 - 396] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) 381. A policy shall specify: (a) The parties between whom the contract is made. (b) The property or life insured. (c) The interest of the insured in property insured, if he is not the absolute owner thereof. (d) The risks insured against. (e) The period during which the insurance is to continue. (f) Either: (1) A statement of the premium, or (2) If the insurance is of a character where the exact premium is only determinable upon the termination of the contract, a statement of the basis and rates upon which the final premium is to be determined and paid. (Enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Insurance Code § 381(a), as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which requires a policy to specify "the parties between whom the contract is made." The original text used definitive language ('agrees to provide and maintain,' 'no less than $5,000,000,' 'requiring that the insurer shall not terminate'), creating a clear obligation for the company to secure insurance. The changed text weakens these obligations ('strive to provide,' 'around $5,000,000,' 'requesting the insurer to provide notice'), introducing ambiguity about the company's commitment to secure the insurance and the amount of coverage, and weakens the requirement for advance notice. The changed text undermines the specificity required by the law, leading to a potential failure to adequately protect the insured parties.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, neither Nantz Communications nor Nantz shall enter into any activity, employment, independent contract, or other business arrangement which conflicts with Nantz Communications' or Nantz's obligations under this Agreement or perform any service which reasonably appears to be an endorsement of the sportswear apparel, hats and shoes of a third party without the Company's prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, Nantz Communications and Nantz should generally avoid activities conflicting with their obligations under this agreement or performing services appearing to endorse sportswear apparel, hats, and shoes of a third party without the Company's approval when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not enter into' to 'should generally avoid' weakens the exclusivity clause. Adding 'appearing to endorse' and 'when feasible' introduces subjective elements and loopholes into the restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow restraints necessary to protect trade secrets may be permissible, the modified language weakens the exclusivity clause to such an extent that it might not provide adequate protection for Ashworth's interests while potentially infringing upon Nantz's ability to pursue other opportunities. The addition of subjective terms like 'generally avoid' and 'when feasible' makes the restriction unenforceable and contradicts the intent to create a binding exclusivity arrangement, creating a situation where Nantz could arguably engage in conflicting activities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the original contract's exclusivity clause, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession. The original text's 'shall not enter into' created a strong restriction, while the changed text's 'should generally avoid' significantly weakens this restriction, introducing ambiguity. The addition of 'appearing to endorse' and 'when feasible' create loopholes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' indicating that any restraint must be narrowly tailored. The weakened language undermines the intended protection of Ashworth's interests and could infringe on Nantz's ability to pursue other opportunities, thus creating a contradiction.
